summary: In 'The Gospel of Wealth', Andrew Carnegie outlines his philosophy on philanthropy, asserting that the wealthy have a moral obligation to distribute their fortunes during their lifetimes. He argues that effective charity should focus on providing the means for self-improvement rather than direct almsgiving, which he believes fails to improve individuals or society.

principles.statement: Wealth must be distributed during one's lifetime for the public good.
principles.rationale: Hoarding wealth until death is a disgrace; actively administering it bridges the gap between the rich and the poor and brings peace to society.
principles.source_quote: The man who dies thus rich dies disgraced.

principles.statement: Charity should empower those who want to help themselves.
principles.rationale: Providing the means for self-improvement is effective, whereas doing everything for someone does not improve the individual or the race.
principles.source_quote: In bestowing charity, the main consideration should be to help those who will help themselves; to provide part of the means by which those who desire to improve may do so

frameworks.name: Effective Philanthropy
frameworks.when_to_apply: When deciding how to distribute wealth or bestow charity.
steps: Identify individuals or groups who have a genuine desire to improve themselves.
Provide part of the means or aids by which they can rise.
Assist them in their efforts, but rarely or never do all the work for them.
frameworks.source_quote: to provide part of the means by which those who desire to improve may do so; to give those who desire to use the aids by which they may rise; to assist, but rarely or never to do all.

mental_models.name: The Gospel of Wealth
mental_models.description: Viewing accumulated wealth not as personal property to be hoarded, but as a trust fund to be actively administered by its possessor during their lifetime for the benefit of the community.
mental_models.example: Giving away wealth during one's lifetime to solve the problem of the Rich and the Poor and bring 'Peace on earth, among men good will.'

heuristics: Help those who will help themselves.
Assist, but rarely or never do all.

signature_quotes.text: The man who dies thus rich dies disgraced.
signature_quotes.context: Carnegie's core belief regarding the hoarding of wealth until death.

signature_quotes.text: In bestowing charity, the main consideration should be to help those who will help themselves...
signature_quotes.context: Guiding principle on how to effectively give to others.

signature_quotes.text: Neither the individual nor the race is improved by almsgiving.
signature_quotes.context: Warning against the negative effects of direct handouts.

anti_patterns.description: Almsgiving or providing direct handouts to individuals.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It does not improve the individual or the race. Truly valuable people rarely require this kind of assistance unless they experience a sudden accident or change.

anti_patterns.description: Dying with a massive, hoarded fortune.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is a disgrace and fails to utilize the wealth to solve societal problems between the rich and the poor during the wealth creator's lifetime.
